decisions.summary: Scope: P0 (shared voice backend) + P1 (Voice Widget) + P2 (AutoPreso View) + P3 (Google Cloud STT fallback). P4 (Windows sidecar) and P5 (TTS agent voice) are deferred to separate issues.
Google Cloud STT ships fully implemented and tested with real GCP credentials. Integration tests are gated on GOOGLE_CLOUD_SPEECH_API_KEY env var and skip when not set.
AutoPreso whiteboard agent ships with all three providers: openai (OPENAI_API_KEY), codex (~/.codex/auth.json bearer), ollama (localhost:11434/v1). All via @ai-sdk/openai adapter.
autopreso source repo (github.com/kunchenguid/autopreso) is MIT licensed — porting code is legally clean.
/ws/voice and /ws/autopreso both use the same noServer+upgrade pattern as ws-terminal.ts — registered before Effect's upgrade listeners in server.ts.
Moonshine ships as packages/moonshine-linux-x64 optional npm dependency containing a PyInstaller-built sidecar binary. The sidecar communicates via JSONL stdin/stdout.
VoiceSettings persisted to ~/.panopticon/voice-settings.json. Hardware device IDs (mic/speaker/volume) stay in browser localStorage (device IDs are machine-specific).
AutoPreso agent provider setting added to existing VoiceSettings type as settings.autopreso.provider. No separate settings file.

hazards.summary: PyInstaller sidecar build is non-trivial: onnxruntime + moonshine-voice Python deps may have conflicts. Build only verified on Linux x64.
hazards.mitigation: The build script creates an isolated venv. If PyInstaller fails, the build script should exit with a clear error message referencing pip freeze output. Document that `npm run build:sidecar` must be run on an Ubuntu x64 machine (or CI runner).

hazards.summary: Moonshine model files are not bundled in the npm package — they must be downloaded separately at runtime. First startup will have latency.
hazards.mitigation: transcription.ts should download the model to ~/.panopticon/moonshine-models/ on first use and emit a 'downloading' event so ws-voice can send a status message to the client. Include a first-run download flow.

hazards.summary: The untracked .claude/skills/pan-tts/scripts/tts_daemon.py in the workspace is a Qwen3-TTS daemon for a different use case (activity log read-aloud) — unrelated to P5 (TTS agent voice). Do NOT include it in this PR.
hazards.mitigation: Work agent must not commit or reference tts_daemon.py. It is a planning artifact from a separate skill.

hazards.summary: Excalidraw controlled mode: when server sends new elements, the Excalidraw component reconciles. In live mode, local user edits to the canvas must not be sent back to the server as authoritative state.
hazards.mitigation: AutoPresoView must not fire the Excalidraw onChange callback to update server state in live mode. Only the whiteboard agent (server-side) updates elements in live mode. Local onChange in live mode should be ignored or used only for viewport commands.

hazards.summary: Browser mic permission: UAT tests for VoiceWidget and AutoPresoView require mic access. The UAT agent must use an isolated Playwright browser profile that has mic permission pre-granted.
hazards.mitigation: In acceptance criteria, specify that UAT must use playwright with --use-fake-ui-for-media-stream flag or grant permission via browser context options. Do not test with a real mic in CI.

hazards.summary: Google Cloud Speech-to-Text StreamingRecognize has a 305-second maximum session limit. Long recordings will be silently cut off.
hazards.mitigation: google-cloud-transcription.ts must restart the streaming session when it approaches the 300s limit. Implement automatic session rotation.

resumePoint.description: Completed voice server wiring implementation for workspace-wrfe; next agent should close already-implemented voice server beads if they become ready, then continue with bd ready -l pan-1053.

sessionHistory.content:
<!-- panopticon:orchestration-context-start -->
<!-- This is Panopticon orchestration context injected automatically.
     It contains planning session setup instructions, not agent reasoning.
     Session summarizers should SKIP this block and focus on the agent's
     actual work, decisions, and tradeoffs that follow. -->

# Planning Session: PAN-1053

## CRITICAL: PLANNING ONLY - NO IMPLEMENTATION

**YOU ARE IN PLANNING MODE. DO NOT:**
- Write or modify any code files (except continue.vbrief.json)
- Run implementation commands (npm install, docker compose, make, etc.)
- Create actual features or functionality
- Start implementing the solution

**YOU SHOULD ONLY:**
- Ask clarifying questions (use AskUserQuestion tool)
- Explore the codebase to understand context (read files, grep)
- Generate planning artifacts:
  - **continue.json** at `.pan/continue.json` — structured decisions, hazards, and approach context (see format below). Replaces the old STATE.md.
  - **vBRIEF plan** at `.pan/spec.vbrief.json` (see format below)
- Present options and tradeoffs for the user to decide

**Finalizing the session:** When your vBRIEF is written and you're ready to hand off, run:

```
pan plan-finalize
```

This converts your `.pan/spec.vbrief.json` into beads tasks and marks the workspace spec as `plan.status = "proposed"`, which lets the dashboard show the **Done** button. Do NOT run `bd create` yourself — `pan plan-finalize` does it deterministically from the vBRIEF.

After `pan plan-finalize` succeeds, STOP. Tell the user: "Planning finalized — click Done in the dashboard to hand off to the implementation agent." Do not kill the tmux session yourself; the Stop button handles that if needed.

## Panopticon Agent Taxonomy

Panopticon orchestrates several distinct agent types. **You are the planning agent.** The other agents in the pipeline have different roles, different working directories, and read different `CLAUDE.md` files. Confusing them is a common error — read this section carefully before exploring the codebase.

### Agents in the Panopticon pipeline

| Agent | Role | Working dir | CLAUDE.md auto-loaded |
|-------|------|-------------|-----------------------|
| **planning** (you) | Discovery, vBRIEF, continue.json. No code. | workspace worktree | workspace |
| **work** | Implementation from your vBRIEF + beads tasks | workspace worktree | workspace |
| **inspect** | Per-bead spec verification mid-implementation — fires only on beads with `metadata.requiresInspection: true` (Jidoka gate; see "Inspection Requirement" below) | project root | project root |
| **review** | Strict code review against acceptance criteria | project root | project root |
| **test** | Test execution and failure analysis | project root | project root |
| **uat** | Browser-based requirement verification (Playwright) | project root | project root |
| **merge** | PR merge, conflict resolution, post-merge cleanup | project root | project root |

**Critical asymmetry:** the workspace `CLAUDE.md` you see is NOT the one specialists see. Specialists run in the project root and auto-load the repo-tracked devroot `CLAUDE.md`. Instructions you put in `continue.json` reach the work agent (same workspace) but not specialists. If you need a specialist to know something, put it in your vBRIEF as an acceptance criterion — that propagates through the pipeline via the role-prompt templates in `src/lib/cloister/prompts/`.

### Claude Code subagents (NOT Panopticon specialists)

You may spawn ephemeral **Claude Code subagents** via the `Agent` tool for parallel exploration. These are NOT the same as Panopticon specialists:

- `codebase-explorer`, `general-purpose` — fast read-only code search
- `Plan` — architectural planning helper
- `code-review-correctness` / `-security` / `-performance` — independent reviewers

**These subagents live and die inside your session.** They have no role in the Panopticon pipeline and no relationship to `review-agent`/`test-agent`/`merge-agent`. If you encounter references in the codebase to "Explorer agent", "explore subagent", `subagent:*` work types, or files in `.claude/agents/` — those are Claude Code subagents, not Panopticon specialists. Do not conflate them.

### What happens after you finalize

After `pan plan-finalize` and the user clicks **Done**, the pipeline runs without you: work agent → inspect (only on flagged beads) → review → test → uat → merge. You are responsible for the plan, not the implementation. Make your vBRIEF and acceptance criteria sharp enough that the work agent can succeed without coming back to you for clarification, and so specialists downstream have unambiguous targets to verify against.

---

## Planning Effort: High (Deep Analysis)

**The user has requested HIGH effort planning.** Be exceptionally thorough:
- Explore more of the codebase before concluding — check adjacent files, not just the obvious ones
- Identify edge cases, potential failure modes, and risks
- Consider multiple implementation approaches and explain tradeoffs
- Ask more clarifying questions when scope is ambiguous
- Break down tasks into finer-grained subtasks


## Issue Details
- **ID:** PAN-1053
- **Title:** feat: Voice STT + AutoPreso whiteboard — Moonshine primary, Google Cloud STT fallback
- **URL:** https://github.com/eltmon/panopticon-cli/issues/1053

## Description
## Summary

Add two related features that share the same voice/STT infrastructure:

1. **Voice STT Widget** — A collapsible voice input widget inside `ComposerFooter`. Speak → transcript inserted into the conversation composer (Edit mode) or sent directly (Direct mode). Moonshine (on-device ONNX) primary, Google Cloud Speech-to-Text fallback.

2. **AutoPreso View** — A first-class dashboard view (`/autopreso`) that is a voice-driven AI whiteboard. Speak → Excalidraw canvas auto-draws the concepts you're describing. Shares 100% of the voice/STT backend with the widget. OpenAI/Codex/Ollama as the whiteboard agent.

Both features land together because they are the same system. The voice pipeline is shared infrastructure; the widget and the whiteboard are two different output surfaces.

---

## Motivation

- **Hands-free coding**: Talk through a change, have it transcribed and sent to a work agent without reaching for the keyboard
- **Voice-driven diagramming**: Speak and have an Excalidraw canvas auto-build diagrams, flowcharts, and architecture sketches in real time — useful for async design discussions, PRD sketching, and live pairing
- **Dogfooding**: Use Panopticon's own voice→agent pipeline on itself
- **Local-first**: Moonshine runs entirely on-device — no API keys, no internet, no latency for STT
- **Cross-platform**: Google Cloud STT fallback ensures the feature works everywhere (Linux now, Windows via Google Cloud STT as quick follow-up)

---

## Shared Infrastructure

Both the Voice Widget and AutoPreso View are clients of the same voice backend:

```
Browser microphone (24kHz PCM)
    │
    │ WebAudio API: AudioContext + ScriptProcessorNode
    │   Resamples any mic sample rate → 24kHz mono PCM
    │
    ▼
/ws/voice WebSocket (raw WS, same pattern as /ws/terminal)
    │
    ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────► Moonshine Python sidecar
    │                                                                       (spawned by Node)
    │                                                               JSONL stdin/stdout
    │                                                               OnnxRuntime inference
    │                                                        ◄──── {type:"transcript:partial"}
    │                                                        ◄──── {type:"transcript:committed"}
    │                                                                
    ◄─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    │
    ├────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┐
    │                            │                                  │
    ▼                            ▼                                  ▼
VoiceWidget                AutoPreso View                  Google Cloud STT
(ComposerFooter)           (/autopreso route)             (fallback provider)
   │                            │                                   │
   │ partial → preview         │ transcript → whiteboard agent      │
   │ committed → editor/send   │ → Excalidraw tool calls           │
   └────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Backend modules (shared)

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `src/dashboard/server/ws-voice.ts` | Raw WebSocket server on `/ws/voice`. Owns Moonshine child process lifecycle. Same `noServer` upgrade pattern as `ws-terminal.ts`. |
| `src/voice/transcription.ts` | `createMoonshineTranscription()` — spawns sidecar binary, JSONL protocol, stdout parsing, ready/close. |
| `src/voice/google-cloud-transcription.ts` | Google Cloud Speech-to-Text streaming — fallback STT provider. |
| `src/voice/turn-queue.ts` | Debounce (150ms), filler-word filter (`isTrivialTranscript`), coalesces buffered chunks into one turn. |
| `src/voice/transcription-manager.ts` | Hot-swappable provider factory. `applyCurrent()` rebuilds active provider at runtime without restart. |
| `src/dashboard/server/routes/voice.ts` | Effect HTTP route for voice settings (`GET /api/voice/settings`, `PUT /api/voice/settings`). |

### Sidecar packaging

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `packages/moonshine-linux-x64/package.json` | npm package, OS: linux, CPU: x64. Optional dependency. |
| `packages/moonshine-linux-x64/bin/moonshine-sidecar` | PyInstaller-built static binary (Ubuntu x64). |
| `scripts/build-moonshine-sidecars.js` | Extend to target `linux-x64` in addition to existing darwin arm64/x64. Build on CI (Ubuntu runner). |

---

## Feature 1: Voice STT Widget

### Integration point

`ComposerFooter` (already handles both agent conversations and issue conversations). The widget is collapsible — collapsed state is just a mic button in the toolbar.

### UX

**Collapsed** (mic button only):
```
[Model▾]  [Effort▾]            [🎤]  [Send ▶]
```

**Expanded**:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  🎤 Mic: [Built-in Microphone ▾]        Model: [medium ▾]     │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ ~~~~~~~~~~~~~ waveform (live AnalyserNode) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~│  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│  "so I want you to refactor the authentic—"                     │
│                                                                    │
│  [Edit | ●Direct]   [ ⏹ Stop ]   [ ✕ Cancel ]  [ ⏎ Send ]  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Send modes**:
- **Edit mode** (default): Live partial transcript shown in preview. Stop → text inserted into Lexical editor. User edits, presses Enter to send. Full optimistic UI, image attachments, model switching all work.
- **Direct mode**: Sends immediately on `transcript:committed` without inserting into editor. No preview.

**Mic button states**: idle (mic icon) → listening (red circle + waveform) → processing (spinner) → error (red dot).

---

## Feature 2: AutoPreso View

### Integration point

New route `/autopreso` in the dashboard. A new panel/tab accessible from the main nav alongside CommandDeck, Kanban, Agents, Resources.

### Architecture (ported from autopreso)

```
Browser mic → /ws/voice → TranscriptionManager → turn queue
                                                         │
                                                         ▼
                                          runWhiteboardAgent (AI SDK tools)
                                                         │
                                                         ▼
                                          whiteboard:apply (line-numbered ops)
                                                         │
                                                         ▼
                                          Excalidraw canvas ← broadcasts
```

### Backend modules (AutoPreso-specific)

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `src/autopreso/session.ts` | `createWhiteboardSession()` — staging/live state machine, warmup loop, `canvasDirtyForAgent` flag |
| `src/autopreso/whiteboard-tools.ts` | `applyWhiteboardEditOperations()`, `formatLineNumberedWhiteboard()` — line-numbered text view |
| `src/autopreso/whiteboard-keywords.ts` | Extract text labels from staging elements as transcription bias keywords |
| `src/autopreso/whiteboard-elements.ts` | Excalidraw element normalization, malformed layout warnings |
| `src/autopreso/agent.ts` | `runWhiteboardAgent()`, `runWhiteboardWarmupOnce()`, system prompt, AI SDK `tool()` schemas |
| `src/dashboard/server/routes/autopreso.ts` | Effect HTTP routes: `POST /api/autopreso/start`, `POST /api/autopreso/back-to-staging`, `POST /api/autopreso/session/reset` |

### Frontend (AutoPreso-specific)

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `src/dashboard/frontend/src/components/autopreso/AutoPresoView.tsx` | Main view — Excalidraw canvas + control panel |
| `src/dashboard/frontend/src/components/autopreso/TranscriptPanel.tsx` | Live transcript display, mode/status indicators |
| `src/dashboard/frontend/src/components/autopreso/AutoPresoControls.tsx` | Mic button, warmup status, staging/live toggle, agent instructions textarea |
| `src/dashboard/frontend/src/hooks/useAutoPresoWebSocket.ts` | Audio capture + /ws/voice WebSocket + /ws/autopreso WebSocket |

### Two-mode session model (from autopreso)

- **`staging`** — Client-side scratchpad. User draws reference content before going live. Server does not track elements in this mode.
- **`live`** — Server owns `state.elements` as source of truth. Audio flows in → transcript → whiteboard agent → Excalidraw updates → broadcast to all clients.

Transition: `POST /api/autopreso/start` snapshots staging primer (elements + screenshot), extracts keywords, kicks off warmup loop.

### Whiteboard agent (AI SDK `tool()` calls)

Two tools, defined inline in `agent.ts` alongside the system prompt (following autopreso's colocated pattern):

- **`whiteboard_apply`**: `replace`, `insert_after`, `delete` operations against line numbers. Optional viewport command (scroll_to_content, zoom, etc.). All in one call.
- **`whiteboard_overwrite`**: Complete replacement of canvas state. Only for clear/reset.

### Prompt cache warmup loop

Before the user speaks, fires 8 warmup attempts with exponential backoff (2s → 4s → 8s → 16s → 30s...). After loop ends, `agentHistory` is forced to `[warmup_user_msg, assistant("UNDERSTOOD")]` so every subsequent turn hits the same cached prefix. This is the real latency trick — the first real transcript turn is already cached.

### Agent providers

Three providers, all via `@ai-sdk/openai` adapter (same as autopreso):
- **openai** — Direct API key
- **codex** — Reads `~/.codex/auth.json`, bearer token to ChatGPT backend
- **ollama** — `http://localhost:11434/v1` (OpenAI-compatible)

---

## STT / TTS Provider Stack

### STT

| Priority | Provider | Type | Latency | Privacy | API Keys |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st** | **Moonshine** (Useful Sensors) | On-device ONNX | Sub-200ms | 100% local | None |
| **2nd** | **Google Cloud Speech-to-Text** | Cloud streaming | ~150ms | Cloud | GCP key |

### TTS (for agent voice — future)

| Priority | Provider | Type | Privacy | API Keys |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st** | **Moonshine TTS** | On-device ONNX | 100% local | None |
| **2nd** | **OpenAI TTS** | Cloud | Cloud | OpenAI key |

### Settings model (backend-persisted, `~/.panopticon/`)

```json
{
  "voice": {
    "stt": {
      "provider": "moonshine",
      "moonshine": { "model": "medium" },
      "googleCloud": { "model": "latest_long" }
    },
    "tts": {
      "provider": "openai",
      "openai": { "model": "tts-1", "voice": "alloy" }
    }
  }
}
```

**Hardware settings** (localStorage only — device IDs change per machine):
- `voiceInputDeviceId`
- `voiceOutputDeviceId`
- `voiceOutputVolume`

---

## Key integration points

### ComposerFooter changes (Voice Widget)

- `VoiceWidget` rendered inside `composerToolbar` div, collapsible via `isVoiceWidgetOpen` state
- Mic button: `<button className={styles.voiceButton} onClick={toggleVoiceWidget}>`
- Widget receives `conversation`, `onSend` (prefills editor), `onSendDirect`
- `handleSubmit()` unchanged — voice text flows through existing optimistic UI path

### Dashboard routing

- New route: `/autopreso` renders `AutoPresoView`
- Main nav gets a new "AutoPreso" entry (microphone icon)
- AutoPreso uses its own WebSocket (`/ws/autopreso`) for whiteboard updates (separate from `/ws/voice` which handles audio→transcript only)

### Moonshine sidecar wire protocol (JSONL, shared)

**Node → sidecar:**
```json
{"type":"audio","encoding":"pcm16le","sampleRate":24000,"audio":"<base64>"}
{"type":"stop"}
```

**Sidecar → Node:**
```json
{"type":"ready"}
{"type":"transcript:partial","text":"so I want you to refac"}
{"type":"transcript:committed","text":"so I want you to refactor the authentication module"}
```

---

## Milestones

### P0 — Shared voice/STT backend + Linux sidecar

- [ ] `src/dashboard/server/ws-voice.ts` — raw WebSocket, Moonshine child process lifecycle
- [ ] `src/voice/transcription.ts` — `createMoonshineTranscription()`
- [ ] `src/voice/turn-queue.ts` — debounce + filler-word filter
- [ ] `src/voice/transcription-manager.ts` — hot-swappable provider factory
- [ ] `src/dashboard/server/routes/voice.ts` — voice settings API
- [ ] `packages/moonshine-linux-x64/` — Linux x64 PyInstaller binary + npm package
- [ ] `scripts/build-moonshine-sidecars.js` — extend to Linux x64 target

### P1 — Voice Widget in ComposerFooter

- [ ] `VoiceWidget.tsx` — collapsible widget with waveform, transcript preview, send modes
- [ ] `useVoiceTranscription.ts` — WebSocket hook for `/ws/voice`
- [ ] Integration into `ComposerFooter` toolbar
- [ ] Edit + Direct send modes
- [ ] Backend voice settings wired to frontend

### P2 — AutoPreso View

- [ ] `src/autopreso/session.ts` — `createWhiteboardSession()` (staging/live, warmup loop)
- [ ] `src/autopreso/whiteboard-tools.ts` — line-numbered ops, apply operations
- [ ] `src/autopreso/whiteboard-keywords.ts` — keyword extraction from staging elements
- [ ] `src/autopreso/whiteboard-elements.ts` — Excalidraw element normalization
- [ ] `src/autopreso/agent.ts` — `runWhiteboardAgent()`, system prompt, AI SDK tools
- [ ] `src/dashboard/server/routes/autopreso.ts` — preso start/back-to-staging/reset routes
- [ ] `AutoPresoView.tsx` — Excalidraw + control panel
- [ ] `TranscriptPanel.tsx` — live transcript display
- [ ] `AutoPresoControls.tsx` — mic, warmup status, staging/live toggle, agent instructions
- [ ] `/ws/autopreso` WebSocket for whiteboard state broadcast
- [ ] `useAutoPresoWebSocket.ts` — combined audio capture + whiteboard state hook
- [ ] Dashboard routing: `/autopreso` route + nav entry

### P3 — Google Cloud STT fallback

- [ ] `src/voice/google-cloud-transcription.ts` — Cloud STT streaming
- [ ] Hot-swap in `createTranscriptionManager`
- [ ] Settings UI: provider picker (Moonshine / Google Cloud)

### P4 — Windows support

- [ ] Windows x64 PyInstaller build
- [ ] `packages/moonshine-windows-x64/` npm package
- [ ] CI: Windows runner for sidecar builds

### P5 — TTS (agent voice)

- [ ] `src/voice/tts.ts` — Moonshine TTS + OpenAI TTS providers
- [ ] Streaming TTS playback via Web Audio API
- [ ] Dashboard setting for agent voice output

---

## References

- autopreso source: https://github.com/kunchenguid/autopreso
- Moonshine / ASR: https://github.com/usefulsensors/moonshine
- Google Cloud Speech-to-Text: https://cloud.google.com/speech-to-text/docs/streaming-best-practices
- Excalidraw: https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw
- AI SDK `tool()`: https://sdk.vercel.ai/docs/reference/ai-sdk-core/tool-calling

---

## Your Mission

You are a planning agent conducting a **discovery session** for this issue.

### Phase 1: Understand Context
1. **If a spec file was provided above**, read it thoroughly — it's your primary input
2. Read the codebase to understand relevant files and patterns
3. Identify what subsystems/files this issue affects
4. Note any existing patterns we should follow

### Phase 2: Discovery Conversation
Use AskUserQuestion tool to ask contextual questions:
- What's the scope? What's explicitly OUT of scope?
- Any technical constraints or preferences?
- What does "done" look like?
- Are there edge cases we need to handle?
- **Are there foundational decisions later beads will depend on?** Flag those for `metadata.requiresInspection: true` (see "Inspection Requirement" below). The rest default to `false`.

### Playwright Isolation

If the issue will require browser-based verification, encode that expectation clearly in continue.json and acceptance criteria:
- Playwright/browser verification must use an isolated browser instance/profile.
- Agents must not depend on another agent's Playwright session or shared browser state.
- Any required login/setup should be reproducible inside the isolated session.

### Task Granularity — Decompose Aggressively

**Default to the smallest bead you can defend.** Your job is to produce a *lot* of small, independently reviewable beads — not a handful of large ones.

A well-sized bead has all of these properties:
- **One focused change.** One command added, one file moved, one collapsed handler, one rename batch. If you need the word "and" in the title, it's probably two beads.
- **Independently reviewable.** A reviewer can verify the acceptance criteria by reading the diff for this bead alone, without cross-referencing others.
- **Independently mergeable.** Landing this bead on its own leaves the tree in a working state. If it can only ship as part of a set, it's a sub-step inside a larger bead, not a bead itself.
- **Testable in isolation.** The acceptance criteria name a specific behavior you can exercise after this bead and no others.

**When a PRD has phases, phases are NOT bead boundaries.** Phases are organizational scaffolding for humans reading the PRD. A single phase will typically decompose into many beads. For example, a phase that says "rename 10 commands" is 10 beads (or 10 sub-items under one rename bead), not 1.

**Concrete heuristics:**
- One collapsed command = one bead. (`pan show`, `pan review`, `pan issues`, `pan plan finalize` → four beads, not one.)
- One renamed verb = one bead, unless several renames are mechanically identical and land in the same file — then they can be sub-items under one bead.
- One admin group moved under a new namespace = one bead per group.
- One distributed-skill rename batch = one bead per logical group (lifecycle shortcuts, admin namespace, umbrella skill, description rewrite sweep). Not one bead for "rename all skills."
- One snapshot test = one bead.
- One doc migration = one bead (per doc or per logical doc cluster, not one bead for "update all docs").

**When in doubt, split.** The cost of too-small beads is mild (more rows to track); the cost of too-large beads is severe (reviewers can't reason about them, work agents deliver partial results, specialists can't pinpoint which acceptance criterion failed, and the `inspect` specialist can't verify mid-implementation). Err on the side of more beads.

**What this does NOT mean:**
- It does NOT mean ship partial features. CLAUDE.md's "Deliver Complete Features" rule still applies: every bead's acceptance criteria must be fully met before it's marked done, and every bead in the plan must ship before the issue itself is marked done. Decomposition is about *reviewability and verifiability*, not about scope reduction.
- It does NOT mean creating beads for trivia that doesn't need tracking (e.g. "update one line in a comment"). If the acceptance criterion fits inside another bead's existing scope and tests, absorb it as a sub-item instead of inflating the bead count.

If the user ever asks "should this be one bead or many?", the answer is almost always "many" unless you can point to a specific reason the work is genuinely indivisible (e.g. a single atomic rename that touches N call sites in one commit).

### Difficulty Estimation

For each sub-task, estimate difficulty using this rubric:

| Level | When to Use | Model |
|-------|-------------|-------|
| `trivial` | Typo, comment, formatting only | haiku |
| `simple` | Bug fix, single file, obvious change | haiku |
| `medium` | New feature, 3-5 files, standard patterns | sonnet |
| `complex` | Refactor, migration, 6+ files, some risk | sonnet |
| `expert` | Architecture, security, performance, high risk | opus |

### Inspection Requirement — `metadata.requiresInspection`

**For every bead, decide whether it needs the inspect-specialist gate before subsequent beads can start.** This is a deliberate, per-bead decision — not a default-on, not a default-off. The decision is recorded as `metadata.requiresInspection: true|false` on each plan item.

**Why this exists:** PAN-382 introduced the inspect specialist after MIN-796, where an agent built `KaiaRuntime.ts` on the wrong foundation (React state machine instead of HTTP/SSE service). That single wrong foundation infected 7 subsequent beads — about 5,800 lines that all had to be redone. Bead-level inspection is Panopticon's Jidoka gate: stop the line at each step, never pass a foundation defect downstream.

**But it's not free.** Per-bead inspection adds wall-clock time and cost to every step. Applying it indiscriminately turns a 12-bead refactor into a 12-step interview. Apply it only where its absence would let a structural defect cascade.

**Set `requiresInspection: true` when ANY of the following are true for this bead:**

1. **Foundation for downstream beads.** Subsequent beads depend on this bead's interfaces, types, file layout, or module boundaries. A wrong choice here is *recoverable only* by redoing the dependent beads. (e.g., "create the runtime layer that all message handling sits on top of," "introduce the new state machine other components will subscribe to.")
2. **Architectural decision crystallizing in code.** The bead encodes a decision the team would want to second-guess at a checkpoint — naming a public API, choosing a library boundary, picking an event shape that other beads will produce or consume.
3. **Spec ambiguity risk.** The bead's description is broad enough that the agent could plausibly produce two very different diffs that both look "done" — the inspector earns its keep by pinning down which one matches the spec.
4. **Security/permission/auth surface.** The bead touches a security boundary, sandbox, or trust gate. Defects propagating into later beads are expensive to unwind once dependent code assumes the security posture.
5. **Cross-cutting protocol or schema.** Wire format, database schema migration, RPC contract, event payload — anything where the *next* bead encodes assumptions about *this* bead's output.

**Set `requiresInspection: false` (the default for most beads) when:**

- The bead is mechanically simple — flag flip, value rename, single-line config change, one-liner bug fix.
- The bead is a leaf — no other bead depends on its internal structure, only on the fact that it shipped.
- The bead is a test, doc, or comment-only update.
- A wrong implementation would surface immediately at typecheck, lint, the verification gate, or end-of-MR review — not as silent foundation rot.
- The bead is part of a parallel batch of mechanically identical operations (10 provider flips, 12 doc renames) where each one's correctness is independently obvious.

**Heuristic shortcut:** if you would expect the inspect specialist to read a 15-line diff and respond "yes that matches the bead description" with no judgment call, set `requiresInspection: false`. Inspection's value is in catching the *judgment-call* defects, not in rubber-stamping mechanical ones.

**You MUST set this field explicitly on every bead.** Omitting it is a planning error — the work prompt requires it. Default to `false` for the typical mechanical bead; flip to `true` only when one of the criteria above genuinely applies. Most plans will have 0–2 beads with `requiresInspection: true`. If a plan has more than 3, ask yourself whether you've under-decomposed — large beads are more often the actual problem.

### Phase 3: Generate Artifacts (NO CODE!)
When discovery is complete:
1. Create **continue.json** at `.pan/continue.json` with decisions, hazards, and approach context (see format below).
2. Create a **vBRIEF plan** at `.pan/spec.vbrief.json` — **MUST follow the exact format below**.
3. Run `pan plan-finalize` from the workspace root. This creates beads tasks from your vBRIEF and sets `plan.status` to `proposed`.
4. Summarize the plan and STOP

**DO NOT run `bd create` commands directly.** `pan plan-finalize` is the only sanctioned way to materialize beads from a workspace vBRIEF plan — it's deterministic and idempotent.

### vBRIEF Plan Format (REQUIRED)

The plan file MUST conform to vBRIEF v0.5 spec (https://github.com/deftai/vBRIEF).
It MUST have exactly two top-level keys: `vBRIEFInfo` and `plan`.

```json
{
  "vBRIEFInfo": {
    "version": "0.5",
    "created": "<ISO 8601 timestamp>",
    "author": "panopticon-cli/0.0.0",
    "description": "Plan for PAN-1053: <issue title>"
  },
  "plan": {
    "id": "pan-1053",
    "title": "<issue title>",
    "status": "approved",
    "uid": "<generate a UUID v4>",
    "author": "agent:claude-opus-4-7",
    "sequence": 1,
    "created": "<ISO 8601 timestamp — same as vBRIEFInfo.created>",
    "updated": "<ISO 8601 timestamp — same as created>",
    "references": [
      { "uri": "https://github.com/eltmon/panopticon-cli/issues/1053", "label": "PAN-1053", "type": "issue" }
    ],
    "tags": ["<relevant tags>"],
    "narratives": {
      "Problem": "<what problem this solves>",
      "Proposal": "<the approach chosen>"
    },
    "items": [
      {
        "id": "<short-kebab-id>",
        "title": "<task title>",
        "status": "pending",
        "priority": "medium",
        "created": "<ISO 8601 timestamp>",
        "metadata": {
          "difficulty": "trivial|simple|medium|complex|expert",
          "issueLabel": "pan-1053",
          "requiresInspection": false
        },
        "narrative": { "Action": "<what needs to be done>" },
        "subItems": [
          {
            "id": "<parent-id>.ac1",
            "title": "<specific testable acceptance criterion>",
            "status": "pending",
            "metadata": { "kind": "acceptance_criterion" }
          }
        ]
      }
    ],
    "edges": [
      { "from": "<source-item-id>", "to": "<target-item-id>", "type": "blocks" }
    ]
  }
}
```

**CRITICAL vBRIEF rules:**
- The file MUST have `vBRIEFInfo` and `plan` as the ONLY top-level keys
- `plan.id` MUST be the issue ID in lowercase (e.g., "pan-1053")
- `plan.uid` MUST be a freshly generated UUID v4
- Do NOT use `issue`, `issueId`, or `issue_id` — use `plan.id`
- `items[].status` MUST be one of: draft, proposed, approved, pending, running, completed, blocked, cancelled
- Acceptance criteria MUST be `subItems` with `metadata.kind: "acceptance_criterion"`
- `metadata.difficulty`, `metadata.issueLabel`, and `metadata.requiresInspection` are Panopticon extensions to the vBRIEF spec
- `metadata.requiresInspection` is REQUIRED on every plan item — see the "Inspection Requirement" section above for the decision criteria. Default to `false` unless the bead lays a foundation other beads depend on, encodes an architectural decision, has spec ambiguity, touches a security/auth boundary, or defines a cross-cutting protocol/schema.
- Edge types: `blocks` (hard dependency), `informs` (soft), `invalidates`, `suggests`

### continue.vbrief.json Format

The continue file is a **structured replacement for STATE.md**. It lives at `.pan/continue.json` and is copied to the lifecycle continue file (`./vbrief/proposed/continue-{issue-id}.vbrief.json`) when planning completes.

```json
{
  "version": "1",
  "issueId": "PAN-1053",
  "created": "<ISO 8601 timestamp>",
  "updated": "<ISO 8601 timestamp>",
  "gitState": { "branch": "<current branch>", "sha": "<short sha>", "dirty": false },
  "decisions": [
    { "id": "D1", "summary": "<decision text>", "recordedAt": "<ISO 8601 timestamp>" }
  ],
  "hazards": [
    { "id": "H1", "summary": "<risk/edge case>", "mitigation": "<how to handle it>" }
  ],
  "resumePoint": null,
  "beadsMapping": {},
  "agentModel": "agent:claude-opus-4-7",
  "sessionHistory": [
    { "timestamp": "<ISO 8601 timestamp>", "reason": "planning", "note": "Initial planning session", "agentModel": "agent:claude-opus-4-7" }
  ]
}
```

**Continue file rules:**
- `version` MUST be `"1"`
- `issueId` MUST match the issue ID in UPPERCASE (e.g., "PAN-1053")
- `decisions` — every architectural or scope decision you make goes here. Future agents (work, review, merge) read these.
- `hazards` — risks, edge cases, and gotchas the work agent should watch for.
- `resumePoint` — leave as `null` during planning; the work agent will populate it.
- `beadsMapping` — leave as `{}`; `pan plan-finalize` populates it when creating beads.
- `sessionHistory` — start with one entry for this planning session.

**Remember:** Be a thinking partner, not an interviewer. Ask questions that help clarify.

Start by exploring the codebase to understand the context, then begin the discovery conversation.
